question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Treat this as a security impacting, cross team incident that requires 2nd level (Identity / Security / Network / Endpoint) response and IR coordination. The pattern best explained by the supplied records is: (A) credential harvesting PDFs that exfiltrate credentials to algina[.]co; (B) emergency sign in / CA / firewall changes applied to contain the phishing that then blocked non MFA interactive users and broke legacy/non interactive service logins; (C) a perimeter ACL/NAT or firewall change that made the partner’s uploads time out even though internal hosts could still reach the file server; and (D) Teams camera losses that match per device OS/permission problems rather than a hardware failure or a single endpoint driver push. This combination needs 2nd level identity + security + network + endpoint teams to coordinate immediately

Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction. REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The incident has operational, diagnostic, and governance implications: token-format compatibility, MFA delegation behavior, increased logging/auditing complexity, and the need for tighter registration/configuration controls and runbooks for ADFS client lifecycle and MFA expectation management. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,237,95); Relationships (390,370,65)] ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration for the new Toolbox MFA client The authentication failures were traced to a newly introduced client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set in a way that changed the authentication flow and produced unexpected token handling and MFA delegation. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. This establishes the registration entry as the high-value control point for preventing recurrence. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. The documentation explicitly links Toolbox to the new client and describes how those client settings led to the observed authentication issues. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65)] The eventual identification of the misconfigured client registration demonstrates that configuration drift (or undocumented registration differences) between environments can mask root causes and delay remediation. Tighter deployment controls, registration promotion processes, and automated configuration comparisons between environments would reduce time-to-resolution. [Data: Entities (248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## ADFS client registration is the primary governance control point and needs stronger lifecycle and validation controls The ADFS client registration object contains the parameters that directly control token format and MFA delegation; the incident shows that a single registration entry can change authentication semantics across an integration. The records treat the client registration as the configuration unit used to represent the new client in ADFS and as the locus for remediation. [Data: Entities (248,249,21); Relationships (390)] This implies governance actions: versioned registration templates, pre-acceptance checks for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders, mandatory compatibility tests per relying party (SAML vs JWT), and an approval workflow for registration changes. These controls will reduce the chance that a single mis-set parameter can cause production authentication failures. [Data: Entities (248,21); Relationships (390,379,382)] ## Monitoring, auditing, and runbooks must be updated to handle MFA delegation and token-format changes Because enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA to external providers and enabling EnableJWT changes token format, logging and audit pipelines must be able to correlate external MFA claims and JWT issuance events back to internal sessions and access decisions. The analysis explicitly flags logging and auditing implications when MFA is forwarded to claims providers. [Data: Entities (241,243,242); Relationships (383,416)] Actionable operational changes include: adding telemetry to record when MFA is delegated, instrumenting JWT issuance events, updating SIEM parsing rules to recognize external MFA claim types and JWT indicators, and creating an incident runbook that includes steps for validating client registration parameters, token-format checks, and claims-provider health checks. These measures are required to maintain diagnostic clarity and compliance posture. [Data: Entities (243,241,240); Relationships (383,379)] ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations (actionable and ordered) Immediate actions: revert or align the problematic ADFS client registration parameters in PROD to the known-good TEST values (which was performed during the incident), validate federation data and certificate status, and confirm Toolbox/Train sign-in behavior in a controlled environment before re-enabling changes. The record of remediation includes changing the ADFS client parameters and updating federation data and certificates. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] Short-to-medium term governance: implement mandatory registration review and approval workflows, require token-format compatibility testing (SAML vs JWT) for each relying party, require explicit validation when enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders including test coverage for external claims-provider responses, add SIEM/monitoring rules to surface unexpected token types and delegated MFA events, and codify an ADFS client registration checklist and runbook for escalations. These steps directly address the identified technical faults and operational weaknesses surfaced by the incident. [Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Closure description: Parameters adjusted on ADFS as in the TEST environment. 18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA Dear Employee 7, During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction. REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The incident has operational, diagnostic, and governance implications: token-format compatibility, MFA delegation behavior, increased logging/auditing complexity, and the need for tighter registration/configuration controls and runbooks for ADFS client lifecycle and MFA expectation management. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,237,95); Relationships (390,370,65)] ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration for the new Toolbox MFA client The authentication failures were traced to a newly introduced client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set in a way that changed the authentication flow and produced unexpected token handling and MFA delegation. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. This establishes the registration entry as the high-value control point for preventing recurrence. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. The documentation explicitly links Toolbox to the new client and describes how those client settings led to the observed authentication issues. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65)] The eventual identification of the misconfigured client registration demonstrates that configuration drift (or undocumented registration differences) between environments can mask root causes and delay remediation. Tighter deployment controls, registration promotion processes, and automated configuration comparisons between environments would reduce time-to-resolution. [Data: Entities (248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## ADFS client registration is the primary governance control point and needs stronger lifecycle and validation controls The ADFS client registration object contains the parameters that directly control token format and MFA delegation; the incident shows that a single registration entry can change authentication semantics across an integration. The records treat the client registration as the configuration unit used to represent the new client in ADFS and as the locus for remediation. [Data: Entities (248,249,21); Relationships (390)] This implies governance actions: versioned registration templates, pre-acceptance checks for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders, mandatory compatibility tests per relying party (SAML vs JWT), and an approval workflow for registration changes. These controls will reduce the chance that a single mis-set parameter can cause production authentication failures. [Data: Entities (248,21); Relationships (390,379,382)] ## Monitoring, auditing, and runbooks must be updated to handle MFA delegation and token-format changes Because enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA to external providers and enabling EnableJWT changes token format, logging and audit pipelines must be able to correlate external MFA claims and JWT issuance events back to internal sessions and access decisions. The analysis explicitly flags logging and auditing implications when MFA is forwarded to claims providers. [Data: Entities (241,243,242); Relationships (383,416)] Actionable operational changes include: adding telemetry to record when MFA is delegated, instrumenting JWT issuance events, updating SIEM parsing rules to recognize external MFA claim types and JWT indicators, and creating an incident runbook that includes steps for validating client registration parameters, token-format checks, and claims-provider health checks. These measures are required to maintain diagnostic clarity and compliance posture. [Data: Entities (243,241,240); Relationships (383,379)] ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations (actionable and ordered) Immediate actions: revert or align the problematic ADFS client registration parameters in PROD to the known-good TEST values (which was performed during the incident), validate federation data and certificate status, and confirm Toolbox/Train sign-in behavior in a controlled environment before re-enabling changes. The record of remediation includes changing the ADFS client parameters and updating federation data and certificates. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] Short-to-medium term governance: implement mandatory registration review and approval workflows, require token-format compatibility testing (SAML vs JWT) for each relying party, require explicit validation when enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders including test coverage for external claims-provider responses, add SIEM/monitoring rules to surface unexpected token types and delegated MFA events, and codify an ADFS client registration checklist and runbook for escalations. These steps directly address the identified technical faults and operational weaknesses surfaced by the incident. [Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)]

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

Could you please reopen this ticket? Thank you and best regards Employee 14 18.12.24 00:33 Ticket closed | TI-1385257 Dear Employee 14, Your ticket from 26.11.24 10:07 has been closed. Closure description: Parameters adjusted on ADFS as in the TEST environment. 18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA Dear Employee 7, During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned

Configuration adjusted according to architectural specifications. Function restored. Activities 03.06.25 07:45 Phone report. Issue: External users can no longer log in to the customer portal. URL: https://portal.kf40.ch Browser error message: 502 – Bad Gateway Internally (via internal network) login works. Priority: High (customers affected) 03.06.25 07:52 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 29 Status: In Progress Notes 03.06.25 08:30 Helpdesk checks: • External DNS resolution correct • Certificate valid • HTTPS reachable • Internal backend (app server) reachable Test: External → 502 Internal → works Suspicion: Reverse Proxy / WAF 03.06.25 09:10 Customer adds: A new certificate was installed over the weekend. Additionally, there was a firewall update (security patch). 03.06.25 10:25 Helpdesk checks firewall: • Rule for port 443 present • NAT correct • No blocking visible in logs Load balancer status: Backend server marked as “Healthy”. 03.06.25 11:40 Test with direct IP to Reverse Proxy → works. Test via FQDN → 502 Notable: Header “X-Forwarded-Proto” missing in external request. 04.06.25 09:05 Customer asks whether certificate may be incorrectly bound. Certificate validation: • CN correct • SAN correct • Chain complete No obvious errors. 05.06.25 08:50 Ticket forwarded to Network / Security Team. Assigned group changed to: BU Network & Security 06.06.25 09:30 Taken over by System Engineer 21 WAF analysis: • Policy active • TLS inspection active • HTTP/2 enabled Notable: After patch, default profile was reloaded. 06.06.25 11:15 Test: TLS inspection temporarily disabled → Login works. Inspection re-enabled → 502 again. 09.06.25 10:05 Further analysis: App server expects header: X-Forwarded-Proto: https When TLS inspection is active, header is not forwarded. 09.06.25 14:40 Comparison with architecture documentation performed. Reference in internal document: “Blueprint: Secure Web Publishing – KF40 – Version 3.2” Section 4.3 describes special header forwarding in case of TLS offloading. 10.06.25 08:25 Blueprint document loaded from DMS. Relevant notes: • TLS termination must occur on WAF • Backend expects HTTPS signaling • Custom header rewrite rule required • Inspection policy must not remove HTTP headers • Specific exception defined for portal application 10.06.25 11:45 Configuration comparison with blueprint shows: • Custom header rewrite rule missing • HTTP profile does not match documented version • Inspection template “Default-Strict” active instead of “Portal-App-Template” 12.06.25 Internal coordination: Firewall patch overwrote template. Custom policy was not reassigned. 16.06.25 Configuration reviewed and adjusted according to blueprint. No technical details documented in ticket (security policy). 18.06.25 14:55 External access test successful. Multiple login attempts tested. Monitoring stable for 24 hours. Ticket closure 18.06.25 15:33 Dear Contact Person 19 The root cause was a deviation in the Reverse Proxy configuration from the documented architectural specification following a security patch. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal blueprint documentation. The customer portal is now fully accessible again. Kind regards Employee 21 Notes: 26.11.24 Ticket opened by Employee 16. 26.11.24 Ticket assigned REPORT 2: # TRAIN CE / ADFS Integration: CE Train Server, ADFS Client Registration, and Toolbox MFA This community centers on the TRAIN CE/CE Train server integration that relies on on‑premises ADFS for authentication. A newly registered ADFS client used by the Toolbox to orchestrate MFA had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders enabled in PROD, which caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to forward MFA to external claims providers; those changes broke non‑browser integrations and produced widespread login/connectivity failures across environments. The incident affected the Train CE server, involved remediation actions (repair installation, federation metadata updates, certificate removals), and required coordinated troubleshooting by internal support engineers, the Identity/Architecture team and an external vendor (Avanade). Secondary factors observed during remediation included stale device compliance from hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices and Conditional Access evaluations that contributed to access variability for some users. The investigation produced both an ADFS configuration documentation artifact and guidance in a broader Identity & Authentication blueprint to prevent recurrence and to inform escalation and monitoring practices. ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration (EnableJWT + RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) on a new client The investigation identified a new client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set to true; this caused ADFS to issue JWTs and to delegate MFA to external claims providers, which the downstream Toolbox/Train integration did not expect and therefore failed to authenticate. The new client registration and the parameter choices are explicitly recorded as the root cause and were adjusted to match the TEST environment to resolve the failures [Data: Entities (249,248,240,241); Relationships (390,370)]. Recommendation: enforce a documented client‑registration review process that verifies token format and MFA delegation flags before promoting client registrations to PROD, and add a checklist item to the CI/CD or change request to confirm EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders settings for non‑browser integrations. ## Production impact and remediation steps applied to the CE Train server The CE Train server (TRAIN CE) experienced connectivity and authentication failures across users and environments; remediation steps recorded include a repair installation of the CE Train server (02.12.24), federation metadata updates on ADFS, and removal/rotation of old federation certificates—followed by ADFS parameter changes that restored correct behaviour. These actions and the target system are captured in the incident notes and ticket timeline [Data: Entities (252,235,254,253); Relationships (405,409,410,367)]. Recommendation: codify the validated remediation sequence (repair, federation metadata verification, certificate refresh, client parameter alignment) into a runbook for TRAIN CE integrations so responders can execute a repeatable playbook during future authentication incidents. ## Stakeholder and escalation map: support engineers, identity/architecture, application owners and external vendor involvement Multiple named stakeholders participated in triage and remediation: Employee 21 acted as lead analyst/owner for related incidents and executed configuration changes and device sync operations; Employee 14 reported and coordinated the initial ticket; Employee 24 and Admin appear in the ticket transfer chain; Avanade participated as an external troubleshooting partner; and the affected tenant and application owners are Customer Company 11AG / 11CE TRAIN. The ticket and assignment flows show cross‑team responsibility and vendor engagement during the incident [Data: Entities (232,4,231,10,229,230); Relationships (353,361,391,17,365)]. Recommendation: publish a concise stakeholder contact matrix and an escalation path (first responder/System Engineer -> Identity/Architecture -> Application Owner -> Vendor) and include roles, expected SLAs, and preferred communication channels for TRAIN CE‑class authentication incidents. ## Conditional Access and device sync were contributing factors for some access failures but were not the primary root cause Conditional Access was evaluated and did not present an outright blocking rule for the affected sign‑ins; however, some hybrid Azure AD‑joined devices reported stale or 'unknown' compliance for 6–8 hours, causing the 'Require compliant device' CA policy to evaluate as not satisfied and producing access variability for certain users. Employee 21 triggered manual device synchronization as part of remediation to update device compliance state faster [Data: Entities (683,680,688,684); Relationships (1199,1201,1206,1189)]. Recommendation: implement monitoring/alerts for device‑sync latency and include device synchronization health in incident triage dashboards; where appropriate, create temporary CA policy exceptions documented in change control but tied to a limited remediation window. ## Logging, monitoring and documentation gaps identified and addressed with configuration documentation and blueprint guidance The post‑incident artifacts include an ADFS configuration documentation entry capturing the corrected client registration settings and the investigation details, authored/confirmed by Employee 21, and a separate Identity & Authentication blueprint that prescribes decision trees, token format guidance, and escalation routes. The blueprint highlights the need for correlated logging across ADFS, claims providers and Entra ID to diagnose delegated MFA and JWT/SAML token flows [Data: Entities (244,232,207,219,208); Relationships (364,317,330)]. Recommendation: ensure the ADFS configuration documentation is versioned in the configuration management repository and update monitoring/alerting to collect token issuance events, claims‑provider logs and correlation IDs to shorten future diagnosis time. ## Token format and non‑browser flow mismatch risk (JWT vs SAML) for applications Enabling JWT issuance via EnableJWT changes the token format emitted by ADFS (JWT/OIDC) and requires that consuming applications support and validate JWTs; the blueprint warns that non‑browser integrations and service accounts commonly fail when token formats change unexpectedly. The incident demonstrates this risk where the new client caused JWT issuance that the Train integration could not process [Data: Entities (240,242,215,216); Relationships (379,324,334)]. Recommendation: require application owners to declare supported token formats in application inventories and gate any client registration change that switches token formats with explicit owner approval and a test plan validating non‑browser flows. ## Process and governance weakness: PROD‑only client registration change and insufficient pre‑deployment verification Investigators initially considered and briefly excluded ADFS because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproduced across environments, and only later was the misconfigured new client identified as the root cause—indicating a gap in change control or rollout validation for client registrations in PROD. The ticket timeline and decision notes record that the misconfigured registration was corrected to match TEST [Data: Entities (21,249,251); Relationships (65,228,390)]. Recommendation: implement formal change controls for ADFS client registrations (including peer review and a rollback plan), require a documented mapping between TEST and PROD registration parameters, and add pre‑promotion automated checks that detect risky flags like EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders in non‑browser client contexts. ## Operationalizing the Decision Tree and runbook: where to escalate and what telemetry to collect The Identity & Authentication blueprint includes a decision tree for authentication issues (question flow for browser vs non‑browser, token type checks, MFA delegation checks and escalation criteria). The incident suggests operationalizing that decision tree into an incident runbook covering when to escalate to the Identity/Architecture team, what logs to collect (ADFS token issuance, claims provider events, Entra ID sign‑in and sync logs), and what immediate mitigations to try (client parameter rollback, device sync, certificate checks) [Data: Entities (225,207,220,219); Relationships (322,348,330)]. Recommendation: convert the blueprint decision tree into a one‑page runbook with command snippets for log collection, prioritized diagnostics, and a checklist for safe client parameter changes to shorten mean‑time‑to‑resolution and reduce handoff friction. REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The incident has operational, diagnostic, and governance implications: token-format compatibility, MFA delegation behavior, increased logging/auditing complexity, and the need for tighter registration/configuration controls and runbooks for ADFS client lifecycle and MFA expectation management. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,237,95); Relationships (390,370,65)] ## Root cause: misconfigured ADFS client registration for the new Toolbox MFA client The authentication failures were traced to a newly introduced client application whose ADFS client registration had EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders set in a way that changed the authentication flow and produced unexpected token handling and MFA delegation. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. This establishes the registration entry as the high-value control point for preventing recurrence. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. The documentation explicitly links Toolbox to the new client and describes how those client settings led to the observed authentication issues. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65)] The eventual identification of the misconfigured client registration demonstrates that configuration drift (or undocumented registration differences) between environments can mask root causes and delay remediation. Tighter deployment controls, registration promotion processes, and automated configuration comparisons between environments would reduce time-to-resolution. [Data: Entities (248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## ADFS client registration is the primary governance control point and needs stronger lifecycle and validation controls The ADFS client registration object contains the parameters that directly control token format and MFA delegation; the incident shows that a single registration entry can change authentication semantics across an integration. The records treat the client registration as the configuration unit used to represent the new client in ADFS and as the locus for remediation. [Data: Entities (248,249,21); Relationships (390)] This implies governance actions: versioned registration templates, pre-acceptance checks for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders, mandatory compatibility tests per relying party (SAML vs JWT), and an approval workflow for registration changes. These controls will reduce the chance that a single mis-set parameter can cause production authentication failures. [Data: Entities (248,21); Relationships (390,379,382)] ## Monitoring, auditing, and runbooks must be updated to handle MFA delegation and token-format changes Because enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA to external providers and enabling EnableJWT changes token format, logging and audit pipelines must be able to correlate external MFA claims and JWT issuance events back to internal sessions and access decisions. The analysis explicitly flags logging and auditing implications when MFA is forwarded to claims providers. [Data: Entities (241,243,242); Relationships (383,416)] Actionable operational changes include: adding telemetry to record when MFA is delegated, instrumenting JWT issuance events, updating SIEM parsing rules to recognize external MFA claim types and JWT indicators, and creating an incident runbook that includes steps for validating client registration parameters, token-format checks, and claims-provider health checks. These measures are required to maintain diagnostic clarity and compliance posture. [Data: Entities (243,241,240); Relationships (383,379)] ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations (actionable and ordered) Immediate actions: revert or align the problematic ADFS client registration parameters in PROD to the known-good TEST values (which was performed during the incident), validate federation data and certificate status, and confirm Toolbox/Train sign-in behavior in a controlled environment before re-enabling changes. The record of remediation includes changing the ADFS client parameters and updating federation data and certificates. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] Short-to-medium term governance: implement mandatory registration review and approval workflows, require token-format compatibility testing (SAML vs JWT) for each relying party, require explicit validation when enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders including test coverage for external claims-provider responses, add SIEM/monitoring rules to surface unexpected token types and delegated MFA events, and codify an ADFS client registration checklist and runbook for escalations. These steps directly address the identified technical faults and operational weaknesses surfaced by the incident. [Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)]
