Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 5: # KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization. ## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected. This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)] ## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior. This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)] ## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration. Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)] ## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall) Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture. Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)] ## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window. The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)] ## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection. These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)] ## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs. The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)] ## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination. Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. Closure description: Parameters adjusted on ADFS as in the TEST environment. 18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA Dear Employee 7, During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true: EnableJWT RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails. RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing. The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims. We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved. The ticket includes portal notes, investigative artifacts (PDFs), named customer contact information, and explicit mentions of credential-harvesting and requested mitigations. Timeline metadata shows the ticket was opened 26.06.24, marked In Progress on 05.07.24, and closed 22.07.24. Key artifacts and relationships in the incident include the two PDF attachments, the fake Microsoft login page, and the algina[.]co exfiltration endpoint. [Data: Entities (80,98,97,96,81,82); Relationships (132,158,157,124,125)] ## Incident record (TI-858473) describes a confirmed credential-harvesting phishing campaign. The ticket TI-858473 is explicitly about a 'Hacker Attack Store' incident opened via the Consulting Sample Company portal and includes investigative notes describing credential-harvesting phishing and requested mitigations; it was created 26.06.24, set In Progress 05.07.24, and closed 22.07.24 with closure text 'Incident has been closed.' This record is the primary anchor for the community of artifacts and actions associated with this event. [Data: Entities (80)] Because the ticket text documents the delivery vector (PDFs), the malicious sign-in page, and the exfiltration endpoint, TI-858473 should be treated as the definitive incident dossier for follow‑up, forensic preservation, and governance actions. [Data: Relationships (132)] ## Delivery vector: two PDF attachments automatically downloaded via the CRM/portal. Two PDF files were uploaded/downloaded via the CRM portal and functioned as the initial delivery vector for the phishing links; portal notes indicate those PDFs were automatically downloaded and later blocked/disabled. This establishes a supply-chain/portal-based vector rather than an email-only vector. [Data: Entities (98); Relationships (132,158)] Operational implication: the portal's handling of uploaded files (automatic download behavior and content scanning/blocking) is directly implicated and should be examined as part of the root-cause and prevention workstream. ## Phishing flow: fake Microsoft login page that exfiltrates credentials to algina[.]co then redirects to Microsoft. The phishing webpage mimicked a Microsoft sign-in page and relayed credentials to algina[.]co before redirecting victims to Microsoft to reduce suspicion; investigative notes explicitly identify that credentials submitted to the fake page were transmitted to ALGINA.CO. The stated purpose of the fake page is credential harvesting to enable further attacks against Customer Company 12. [Data: Entities (97,96); Relationships (157)] This implies successful capture of credentials is possible if users entered values, and that captured credentials were actively transmitted off‑network to a hostile internet domain. ## Customer scope and named contact are documented in the ticket. TI-858473 was opened on behalf of Customer Company 12 and names Contact Person 10 as the customer contact and recipient of status communications. Those relationships are recorded in the incident metadata and link the investigative actions and communications to a single tenant and contact point. [Data: Entities (81,82); Relationships (124,125)] For remediation and notification, Customer Company 12 and Contact Person 10 are the primary stakeholders and should be included in any credential-reset, MFA-enforcement, and forensic communication plans. ## Evidence preservation: PDFs and portal notes are part of the incident evidence set. The ticket includes the two PDF attachments and portal notes that document automatic download, blocking/disablement, and investigative observations; these artifacts are captured as part of the incident record and form the evidentiary basis for attribution of the delivery method and phishing payload. [Data: Relationships (132); Entities (98,80)] Maintaining chain-of-custody for these artifacts (original PDFs, portal logs, webserver logs, and any captured exfiltration indicators) is essential for both remediation verification and any legal/abuse reporting to hosting/registrar providers. ## Operational timeline indicates multi-week duration between detection and closure. The recorded timeline shows TI-858473 was opened on 26.06.24, not set In Progress until 05.07.24, and closed on 22.07.24, with priority marked as HIGH. This multi-week lifecycle suggests either investigation complexity, coordination with the customer, or potential process/SLAs that delayed active response. [Data: Entities (80)] Long incident durations increase exposure risk—if credentials were compromised, attackers had time to act—so incident lifecycle performance should be reviewed (detection → triage → containment → remediation) to identify bottlenecks. ## Confirmed exfiltration endpoint (algina[.]co) provides an actionable indicator for blocking and reporting. Investigative notes and relationships identify algina[.]co as the credential exfiltration endpoint used by the fake login flow, making it a concrete IOC (indicator of compromise) for network, DNS, and firewall controls as well as for third-party abuse/registrar reporting. [Data: Entities (96); Relationships (157)] This domain should be prioritized for immediate blocking at perimeter/DNS filtering layers and included in incident reporting to abuse contacts, with logs preserved to track any outbound communication from affected accounts or devices. ## High operational and security significance with immediate remediation needs. Because the event is a credential-harvesting campaign delivered via customer-facing portal artifacts and confirmed to exfiltrate credentials to an external domain, the incident carries high operational and security impact for the affected tenant; the ticket itself is marked HIGH priority and documents requested mitigations. [Data: Entities (80,97,96,98)] The combination of delivery via portal, automatic download behavior, a credible site mimic (Microsoft sign-in), and external exfiltration makes this an event warranting prioritized actions: targeted credential resets, verification/enforcement of MFA on impacted accounts, blocking of the malicious domain, forensic review of portal file handling and access logs, and customer notification/escalation. ## Recommendations: prioritized operational runbook and governance actions. Immediate runbook priorities (recommended): 1) Treat the event as active compromise until proven otherwise: identify accounts that used the portal or clicked the PDFs; enforce password resets and require MFA for those accounts. 2) Block algina[.]co at DNS/proxy/firewall and submit abuse reports to the domain registrar/hosting provider. 3) Quarantine/remove the PDF artifacts from the portal, review portal file handling for automatic-download behavior, and harden content scanning. 4) Preserve evidence (PDFs, portal logs, webserver/DNS logs, firewall logs) for forensic analysis and any external reporting. 5) Review incident SLAs and escalation paths given the multi-week timeline observed and update runbooks to shorten time-to-containment. These recommendations follow directly from the artifacts and flow recorded in the incident (PDFs → fake Microsoft page → algina[.]co) and the documented customer/ticket metadata. context: SOURCE 31: denied again However: Other users WITHOUT “Compliance_Core” membership have access. Hypothesis rejected – not purely group-based. 24.03.25 08:55 Analysis Azure AD Sign-In Logs For affected users: • Token contains claim for “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” • No claim for “Compliance_Core” • Conditional Access evaluation successful No blocking visible. 24.03.25 10:10 In-depth review via Microsoft Purview / Information Protection Notable: When applying the Sensitivity Label, the option “Preserve custom permissions” was not activated. Result: • Existing SharePoint permissions formally remained • Encryption, however, applies only to explicitly defined groups This creates a situation where: SharePoint says → Access allowed AIP encryption says → Access only with key permission Depending on token refresh timing, access is granted or blocked. 24.03.25 14:35 Reproduction in test tenant: • Folder with Unique Permissions created • Sensitivity Label applied afterward • Different users tested Result: Users with older access tokens could temporarily access. After token renewal, access blocked. 25.03.25 09:15 Root cause defined: The folder was: 1. Renamed 2. Assigned Unique Permissions 3. Subsequently encrypted with a Sensitivity Label The encryption defined a different permission group than the SharePoint ACL. Token caching caused inconsistent behavior. 25.03.25 11:40 – Actions • Sensitivity Label removed • Folder reassigned with correct group reference • Label reapplied with correct group integration • “Compliance_Core” and “COMPLIANCE_TEAM” structurally aligned • Tests performed with multiple user accounts • Tokens intentionally invalidated 26.03.25 – Monitoring Multiple users tested Access consistently possible No further error messages 28.03.25 15:42 – Customer feedback Audit team confirms stable access for all employees. Ticket closure 28.03.25 16:02 Dear Contact Person 02 The root cause was an inconsistent combination of Unique SharePoint permissions and a subsequently applied Sensitivity Label encryption. Due to differing group references between ACL and encryption, inconsistent access behavior occurred, amplified by token caching. The permission structure was aligned and tested. Access is now stable.
